SUMMARY:
Air pollution is the presence of substances in the air that are harmful to humans, other living beings or the environment. Pollutants can be gases, like ozone

![icon](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bf/Aegopodium_podagraria1_ies.jpg/20px-Aegopodium_podagraria1_ies.jpg)
![icon](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6a/Earth_Day_Flag.png/20px-Earth_Day_Flag.png)

**Air pollution** is the presence of substances in the [air](/wiki/Atmosphere_of_Earth "Atmosphere of Earth") that are harmful to humans, other living beings or the environment. Pollutants can be [gases](/wiki/Gas "Gas"), like [ozone](/wiki/Ground-level_ozone "Ground-level ozone") or [nitrogen oxides](/wiki/Nitrogen_oxides "Nitrogen oxides"), or small particles like [soot](/wiki/Soot "Soot") and dust. Both outdoor and indoor air can be polluted.

Outdoor air pollution comes from burning [fossil fuels](/wiki/Fossil_fuel "Fossil fuel") for electricity and [transport](/wiki/Transport "Transport"), [wildfires](/wiki/Wildfire "Wildfire"), some industrial processes, waste management, [demolition](/wiki/Demolition "Demolition") and agriculture. Indoor air pollution is often [from burning firewood or agricultural waste](/wiki/Energy_poverty_and_cooking "Energy poverty and cooking") for cooking and heating. Other sources of air pollution include [dust storms](/wiki/Dust_storm "Dust storm") and [volcanic eruptions](/wiki/Volcanic_eruption "Volcanic eruption"). Many sources of local air pollution, especially burning fossil fuels, also release [greenhouse gases](/wiki/Greenhouse_gas "Greenhouse gas") that cause [global warming](/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change"). However air pollution may limit warming locally.

Air pollution kills 7 or 8 million people each year. It is a significant [risk factor](/wiki/Risk_factor "Risk factor") for a number of diseases, including [stroke](/wiki/Stroke "Stroke"), [heart disease](/wiki/Heart_disease "Heart disease"), [chronic obstructive pulmonary disease](/wiki/Chronic_obstructive_pulmonary_disease "Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease") (COPD), [asthma](/wiki/Asthma "Asthma") and [lung cancer](/wiki/Lung_cancer "Lung cancer"). [Particulate matter](/wiki/Particulate_matter "Particulate matter") is the most deadly, both for indoor and outdoor air pollution. Ozone affects crops, and forests are damaged by the pollution that causes [acid rain](/wiki/Acid_rain "Acid rain"). Overall, the [World Bank](/wiki/World_Bank "World Bank") has estimated that welfare losses (premature deaths) and [productivity](/wiki/Productivity "Productivity") losses (lost labour) caused by air pollution cost the [world economy](/wiki/World_economy "World economy") over $8 trillion per year.

Various technologies and strategies reduce air pollution. Key approaches include [clean cookers](/wiki/Clean_cooking "Clean cooking"), [fire protection](/wiki/Fire_protection "Fire protection"), improved [waste management](/wiki/Waste_management "Waste management"), [dust control](/wiki/Dust#Control "Dust"), [industrial scrubbers](/wiki/Scrubber "Scrubber"), [electric vehicles](/wiki/Electric_vehicle "Electric vehicle") and [renewable energy](/wiki/Renewable_energy "Renewable energy"). National air quality laws have often been effective, notably the 1956 [Clean Air Act](/wiki/Clean_Air_Act_1956 "Clean Air Act 1956") in Britain and the 1963 [US Clean Air Act](/wiki/Clean_Air_Act_(United_States) "Clean Air Act (United States)"). International efforts have had mixed results: the [Montreal Protocol](/wiki/Montreal_Protocol "Montreal Protocol") almost eliminated harmful [ozone](/wiki/Ozone_layer "Ozone layer")-depleting chemicals, while [international action on climate change](/wiki/Climate_change_mitigation "Climate change mitigation") has been less successful.

## Sources

### Human sources

#### Industry and construction

![Dust and debris rise as large concrete cooling towers collapse during a controlled demolition](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/Athlone_cooling_towers_demolition_2010-08-22.gif/250px-Athlone_cooling_towers_demolition_2010-08-22.gif)
![A factory with chimneys releasing smoke.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a4/Air_pollution_by_industrial_chimneys.jpg/250px-Air_pollution_by_industrial_chimneys.jpg)

Burning fuel to [generate electricity](/wiki/Electricity_generation "Electricity generation") causes air pollution; [Lignite and coal produce most air pollution](/wiki/Health_and_environmental_impact_of_the_coal_industry "Health and environmental impact of the coal industry"), followed by oil, and then by [fossil gas](/wiki/Natural_gas "Natural gas") and [biomass](/wiki/Biomass "Biomass").[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-OWID_SafestEnergy_2021-2) [Methane leaks](/wiki/Methane_leak "Methane leak") are common in oil and gas production,[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) and [oil refinery](/wiki/Oil_refinery "Oil refinery") emits a wide range of pollutants.[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESinghSingh2019166-5) Some hazardous air pollutants are produced in plastic and rubber production, whereas [chloroform](/wiki/Chloroform "Chloroform") can be produced during [water chlorination](/wiki/Water_chlorination "Water chlorination"), and [arsenic](/wiki/Arsenic_poisoning "Arsenic poisoning") is found in the mining industry.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHill2020103-6) Many polluting industries have been pushed out of richer nations, and China too has started to push its most polluting industries out of the country.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

[Construction](/wiki/Construction "Construction") and [demolition](/wiki/Demolition "Demolition") produces dust, but also other pollutants. The direct particles from construction and demolition are relatively coarse.[[8]](#cite_note-DEFRA_PM-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9) Construction also has an indirect impact on air quality, as [cement](/wiki/Cement "Cement") production is one of the main sources of particle pollution.[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESinghSingh2019166-5) Though banned in many countries, [asbestos](/wiki/Asbestos "Asbestos") persists in older buildings, where it poses a risk of lung disease when disturbed.[[10]](#cite_note-10) Building materials including carpeting and [plywood](/wiki/Plywood "Plywood") emit [formaldehyde](/wiki/Formaldehyde "Formaldehyde"), a gas which can cause difficulty breathing and [nausea](/wiki/Nausea "Nausea").[[11]](#cite_note-:6-11)

#### Transportation

Road vehicles produce a significant amount of all air pollution. For instance, they may be responsible for a third to half of all [nitrogen dioxide](/wiki/Nitrogen_dioxide "Nitrogen dioxide") emissions,[[12]](#cite_note-pmid31143547-12)[[13]](#cite_note-pmid26142107-13) and are a major cause of [climate change](/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change").[[14]](#cite_note-14) Vehicles with petrol and diesel engines produce about half of their emissions from their [exhaust gas](/wiki/Exhaust_gas "Exhaust gas"), and the other half from [non-exhaust emissions](/wiki/Non-exhaust_emissions "Non-exhaust emissions") (tyre and brake wear and erosion or disturbance of the road surface); [electric vehicles](/wiki/Electric_vehicle "Electric vehicle") produce no tailpipe emissions, but still produce the other emissions.[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAmato20184-15) [Diesel trains](/wiki/Diesel_locomotive "Diesel locomotive"), ships and [planes](/wiki/Airplane "Airplane") also cause air pollution.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

#### Agriculture and waste

![Smoke rises from a fire, and is dispersed by wind](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/52/BurningOffFieldsInTheEveningInSouthGeorgia.jpg/250px-BurningOffFieldsInTheEveningInSouthGeorgia.jpg)
![Several people are working on a heap of rubbish, with smoke rising from it.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b7/Agbogbloshie%2C_Ghana_2019.jpg/250px-Agbogbloshie%2C_Ghana_2019.jpg)

[Agricultural emissions](/wiki/Environmental_impact_of_agriculture "Environmental impact of agriculture"), both from crops and from [animal agriculture](/wiki/Environmental_impacts_of_animal_agriculture "Environmental impacts of animal agriculture"), contribute substantially to air pollution.[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-10.1038/nature15371-18) For instance, [methane](/wiki/Methane "Methane") is emitted by the [digestion](/wiki/Digestion "Digestion") of food by [cattle](/wiki/Cattle "Cattle"), causing ground-level ozone.[[19]](#cite_note-19) Agriculture is also a major source of ammonia, which can form fine particulate matter.[[20]](#cite_note-20) Practices like [slash-and-burn](/wiki/Slash-and-burn "Slash-and-burn") in forests like the [Amazon](/wiki/Amazon_rainforest "Amazon rainforest") cause large air pollution alongside [deforestation](/wiki/Deforestation "Deforestation").[[21]](#cite_note-21)

[Open dumps](/wiki/Open_dump "Open dump") of waste are a common source of air pollution in low-income countries. They can promote the growth of microbes which pollute water and air, and be a source of toxins. Through [open burning of waste](/wiki/Open_burning_of_waste "Open burning of waste")—whether [self-ignited](/wiki/Spontaneous_combustion "Spontaneous combustion") or burned on purpose—soot, methane, and other pollutants are released.[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUnited_Nations_Environmental_Programme202137–39-22) The waste in landfills itself also produces methane.[[23]](#cite_note-23) Globally, a quarter of [solid waste](/wiki/Municipal_solid_waste "Municipal solid waste") is not collected and another quarter is not disposed of properly.[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECookVelis20207-24)

#### Household sources

![a round metal open fire with small fish](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a4/Fish_smoking_in_Ghana.jpg/250px-Fish_smoking_in_Ghana.jpg)

As of 2023, more than 2.3 billion people in [developing countries](/wiki/Developing_country "Developing country") rely on burning polluting fuels such as [firewood](/wiki/Firewood "Firewood"), agricultural waste, [dry dung](/wiki/Dry_dung_fuel "Dry dung fuel"), [coal](/wiki/Coal "Coal") or [charcoal](/wiki/Charcoal "Charcoal") for cooking, which causes harmful [household air pollution](/wiki/Household_air_pollution "Household air pollution").[[25]](#cite_note-25) [Kerosine](/wiki/Kerosene "Kerosene"), another polluting fuel, is used in many countries for [lighting](/wiki/Lighting "Lighting") and sometimes for space heating or cooking. Globally, 12% of outdoor fine particle pollution comes from household cooking. Health effects are concentrated among women, who are likely to be responsible for cooking, and young children.[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWorld_Health_Organization2016vii–xi-26)

[Gas stoves](/wiki/Gas_stove "Gas stove") for cooking contribute to indoor air pollution by emitting NO2, [benzene](/wiki/Benzene "Benzene") and carbon monoxide.[[27]](#cite_note-27) [Toasters](/wiki/Toaster "Toaster") can produce particulate pollution.[[28]](#cite_note-28) Similarly, [heating systems](/wiki/Heating_system "Heating system") such as [furnaces](/wiki/Furnace_(central_heating) "Furnace (central heating)") and other types of fuel-burning heating devices release pollutants into the air.[[29]](#cite_note-29) In some developed countries, including the UK and Sydney, Australia, [wood stoves](/wiki/Wood_stoves "Wood stoves") are the major source of particulate pollution in urban areas.[[30]](#cite_note-30)[[31]](#cite_note-auto-31) Wood stoves can also emit carbon monoxide and NO*x*.[[11]](#cite_note-:6-11)

Other sources of indoor air pollution are building materials, biological material and tobacco smoke. Biological material, such as [dander](/wiki/Dander "Dander"), [house dust mite](/wiki/House_dust_mite "House dust mite"), [mold](/wiki/Mold "Mold") and [pollen](/wiki/Pollen "Pollen"), can come from humans, animals or plants. Some of this material can trigger allergies, such as [allergic rhinitis](/wiki/Allergic_rhinitis "Allergic rhinitis").[[11]](#cite_note-:6-11) Fumes from [pesticides](/wiki/Pesticide "Pesticide"), paints, [cleaning products](/wiki/Cleaning_agent "Cleaning agent") and [personal care products](/wiki/Personal_care_products "Personal care products") can be substantial, and make up an increasing share of outdoor air pollution as transportation is getting cleaner.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

### Natural sources

![A dust storm approaches a collection of houses, dwarfing them in height.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d2/Dust_storm_approaching_Stratford%2C_Texas.jpg/250px-Dust_storm_approaching_Stratford%2C_Texas.jpg)

[Dust](/wiki/Dust "Dust") from desert can cause poor air quality far from its source. For instance, dust from the [Gobi Desert](/wiki/Gobi_Desert "Gobi Desert") in China and Mongolia can reach [Hawaii](/wiki/Hawaii "Hawaii"), and dust from the [Sahara](/wiki/Sahara "Sahara") reaches the [Amazon rainforest](/wiki/Amazon_rainforest "Amazon rainforest") in South America.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

[Radon](/wiki/Radon "Radon") is a radioactive gas that can build up in buildings from the [soil](/wiki/Soil "Soil"). It can cause [lung cancer](/wiki/Lung_cancer "Lung cancer"), especially in [smokers](/wiki/Smoking "Smoking"). Levels are generally low, but can be elevated in buildings with "leaky" foundations or areas with soils rich in [uranium](/wiki/Uranium "Uranium").[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarrisonHester201957–58-34) Volcanic eruptions can be a large source of sulfur dioxide and also produces particle pollution.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

[Vegetation](/wiki/Vegetation "Vegetation") can emit gases that contribute to ozone formation and particle pollution. This is especially true in warmer climates and during the growth season.[[36]](#cite_note-36) These gases react with human pollution sources to produce a seasonal haze.[[37]](#cite_note-37) [Black gum](/wiki/Black_gum "Black gum"), poplar, oak and [willow](/wiki/Willow "Willow") emit gases that can raise ozone levels up to eight times more than low-impact tree species.[[38]](#cite_note-38) [Wildfires](/wiki/Wildfire "Wildfire"), which have become more severe and more common due to climate change, release fine particles. They are a major source of air pollution.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

## Major pollutants

![Schematic showing (1) CO2 and CH4 from heating, driving and livestock causing warming (2) PM pollution from driving (3) CFCs from buildings depleting ozone, causing UV to come through (4) SO2 from combustion causing acid rain and (5) ground-level ozone from nitrous oxides and (6) nitrous oxides from car exhausts.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/14/Air_Pollution-Causes%26Effects.svg/330px-Air_Pollution-Causes%26Effects.svg.png)

Air pollutants can be tiny solid or liquid particles dispersed in the air (called [aerosols](/wiki/Aerosol "Aerosol")), or gases.[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPearsonDerwent20221,_6-40) Pollutants are classified as primary or secondary. Primary pollutants are produced directly by a source and remain in the same chemical form after they have been emitted into the atmosphere. Examples include [carbon monoxide](/wiki/Carbon_monoxide "Carbon monoxide") gas from car exhausts, and sulfur dioxide from factories. Secondary pollutants are not emitted directly. Rather, they form in the air when primary pollutants react with each other or with other parts of the atmosphere. [Ground-level ozone](/wiki/Ground-level_ozone "Ground-level ozone") is one example of a secondary pollutant. Some pollutants may be both primary and secondary: they are both emitted directly and formed from other primary pollutants.[[41]](#cite_note-41)

[Ammonia](/wiki/Ammonia_pollution "Ammonia pollution") (NH3) is emitted mainly by overuse of synthetic nitrogen [fertilisers](/wiki/Fertilizer "Fertilizer") on farmland, and from [manure](/wiki/Manure "Manure") and urine from [livestock](/wiki/Livestock "Livestock").[[42]](#cite_note-42) At typical concentrations in the air, it is not harmful to health directly. However, ammonia can react with other pollutants in the air to form [ammonium sulfate](/wiki/Ammonium_sulfate "Ammonium sulfate") or [nitrate](/wiki/Ammonium_nitrate "Ammonium nitrate") salts, contributing to particulate matter pollution. Furthermore, when ammonia is [deposited](/wiki/Deposition_(aerosol_physics) "Deposition (aerosol physics)") onto the soil, it can harm ecosystems via [eutrophication](/wiki/Eutrophication "Eutrophication").[[43]](#cite_note-43)

### Carbon dioxide

[Carbon dioxide](/wiki/Carbon_dioxide "Carbon dioxide") (CO2) is mainly emitted by the burning of fossil fuels.[[44]](#cite_note-44) CO2 is sometimes called an air pollutant, because it is the main [greenhouse gas](/wiki/Greenhouse_gas "Greenhouse gas") responsible for [climate change](/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change").[[45]](#cite_note-45)[[46]](#cite_note-46) Although the [World Health Organization](/wiki/World_Health_Organization "World Health Organization") recognizes CO2 as a climate pollutant, it does not include the gas in its *Air Quality Guidelines* or set recommended targets for it.[[47]](#cite_note-47) This question of terminology has practical consequences, for example, in determining whether the U.S. [Clean Air Act](/wiki/Clean_Air_Act_(United_States) "Clean Air Act (United States)") (which is designed to improve air quality) is deemed to regulate CO2 emissions. The [Inflation Reduction Act](/wiki/Inflation_Reduction_Act "Inflation Reduction Act") of 2022 amended the Clean Air Act to define CO2 from fossil fuel burning explicitly as an air pollutant.[[48]](#cite_note-48)

[Carbon monoxide](/wiki/Carbon_monoxide "Carbon monoxide") (CO) is a colorless, odorless, toxic gas.[[49]](#cite_note-49) It is a product of [combustion](/wiki/Combustion "Combustion") of fuel such as natural gas, coal or wood. ## Air Pollution Definition

***“Air Pollution is the release of pollutants such as gases, particles, biological molecules, etc. into the air that is harmful to human health and the environment.”***

### Air Pollution Diagram

![Air pollution](https://cdn1.byjus.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/05/Air-pollution.png "Air pollution")

**Table of Contents**

## What is Air Pollution?

Air pollution refers to any physical, chemical or biological change in the air. It is the contamination of air by harmful gases, dust and smoke which affects plants, animals and humans drastically.

There is a certain percentage of gases present in the atmosphere. An increase or decrease in the composition of these gases is harmful to survival. This imbalance in the gaseous composition has resulted in an increase in earth’s temperature, which is known as global warming.

## Types of Air Pollutants

There are two types of air pollutants:

### Primary Pollutants

The pollutants that directly cause air pollution are known as primary pollutants. Sulphur-dioxide emitted from factories is a primary pollutant.

### Secondary Pollutants

The pollutants formed by the intermingling and reaction of primary pollutants are known as secondary pollutants. Smog, formed by the intermingling of smoke and fog, is a secondary pollutant.

**Also Read:**[Water Pollution](https://byjus.com/chemistry/water-pollution-causes/)

## Causes of Air Pollution

Following are the important causes of air pollution:

### Burning of Fossil Fuels

The combustion of [fossil fuels](https://byjus.com/biology/fossil-fuel/) emits a large amount of sulphur dioxide. Carbon monoxide released by incomplete combustion of fossil fuels also results in air pollution.

### Automobiles

The gases emitted from vehicles such as jeeps, trucks, cars, buses, etc. pollute the environment. These are the major sources of greenhouse gases and also result in diseases among individuals.

### Agricultural Activities

Ammonia is one of the most hazardous gases emitted during agricultural activities. The insecticides, pesticides and fertilisers emit harmful chemicals in the atmosphere and contaminate it.

### Factories and Industries

Factories and industries are the main source of carbon monoxide, organic compounds, hydrocarbons and chemicals. These are released into the air, degrading its quality.

### Mining Activities

In the mining process, the minerals below the earth are extracted using large pieces of equipment. The dust and chemicals released during the process not only pollute the air, but also deteriorate the health of the workers and people living in the nearby areas.

### Domestic Sources

The household cleaning products and paints contain toxic chemicals that are released in the air. The smell from the newly painted walls is the smell of the chemicals present in the paints. It not only pollutes the air but also affects breathing.

## Effects of Air Pollution

The hazardous effects of air pollution on the environment include:

### Diseases

Air pollution has resulted in several respiratory disorders and heart diseases among humans. The cases of lung cancer have increased in the last few decades. Children living near polluted areas are more prone to pneumonia and asthma. Many people die every year due to the direct or indirect effects of air pollution.

### Global Warming

Due to the emission of greenhouse gases, there is an imbalance in the gaseous composition of the air. This has led to an increase in the temperature of the earth. This increase in earth’s temperature is known as [global warming](https://byjus.com/biology/global-warming/). This has resulted in the melting of glaciers and an increase in sea levels. Many areas are submerged underwater.

### Acid Rain

The burning of fossil fuels releases harmful gases such as nitrogen oxides and sulphur oxides in the air. The water droplets combine with these pollutants, become acidic and fall as acid rain which damages human, animal and plant life.

### Ozone Layer Depletion

The release of chlorofluorocarbons, halons, and hydrochlorofluorocarbons in the atmosphere is the major cause of depletion of the ozone layer. The depleting ozone layer does not prevent the harmful ultraviolet rays coming from the sun and causes skin diseases and eye problems among individuals.  
**Also Read:**[Ozone Layer Depletion](https://byjus.com/biology/ozone-layer-depletion/)

### Effect on Animals

The air pollutants suspend in the water bodies and affect aquatic life. Pollution also compels the animals to leave their habitat and shift to a new place. This renders them stray and has also led to the extinction of a large number of animal species.

## Air Pollution Control

Following are the measures one should adopt, to control air pollution:

### Avoid Using Vehicles

People should avoid using vehicles for shorter distances. Rather, they should prefer public modes of transport to travel from one place to another. This not only prevents pollution, but also conserves energy.

### Energy Conservation

A large number of fossil fuels are burnt to generate electricity. Therefore, do not forget to switch off the electrical appliances when not in use. Thus, you can save the environment at the individual level. Use of energy-efficient devices such as CFLs also controls pollution to a greater level.

### Use of Clean Energy Resources

The use of solar, wind and geothermal energies reduce air pollution at a larger level. Various countries, including India, have implemented the use of these resources as a step towards a cleaner environment.

## Frequently Asked Questions

Q1

### What is the major cause of air pollution?

The main cause of air pollution is the burning of fossil fuels. Harmful gases like sulphur dioxide, carbon monoxide etc. are released into the atmosphere due to incomplete combustion of fossil fuels which pollutes the air.

Q2

### How air pollution causes global warming?

In air pollution, the release of greenhouse gases changes the gaseous composition of the atmosphere and causes an increase in the temperature of the earth. This increased temperature of earth is known as global warming.

Q3

### What is acid rain? Name the gases responsible for acid rain.

Acid rain is the precipitation of acid in the form of rain. The harmful gases like nitrogen oxides and sulphur oxides are released into the atmosphere by burning of fossil fuels. These pollutants react with the rainwater and fall as acid rain.

Q4

### Deforestation is a major reason for air pollution. Explain.

Deforestation can be defined as the large-scale removal of trees from forests or other lands. Plants utilise carbon dioxide (CO2) from the atmosphere for the process of photosynthesis, this causes a decrease in the amount of CO2 in the atmosphere. As the number of trees decline due to deforestation, the amount of CO2 in the atmosphere increases, causing air pollution.

--- SOURCE 5: Air Pollution and Your Health ---
URL: https://www.niehs.nih.gov/health/topics/agents/air-pollution

SUMMARY:
NIEHS research uses state-of-the-art science and technology to investigate the interplay between environmental exposures, human biology, genetics, and common diseases to help prevent disease and improve human health. NIEHS offers a broad range of job opportunities, career enhancement programs, and research training grants and programs in environmental health sciences and administration. NIEHS supported a translational research project,Addressing Air Pollution and Asthma (1MB), that may lead to improved health for children suffering from asthma. Air Pollution Linked to Dementia Cases (September 2023) – In this edition of NIH Research Matters, read about findings from the Health and Retirement Study, funded by the National Institute on Aging, that showed higher air pollution exposure was linked to an increased risk of dementia.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Air pollution is the presence of harmful substances in the atmosphere, including gases and particulate matter, adversely affecting health and the environment.
Addressing air pollution is crucial as it impacts public health, contributes to climate change, and causes economic losses, with millions of premature deaths annually.
Control technologies include renewable energy, industrial scrubbers, clean cookers, and electric vehicles, aimed at reducing emissions and improving air quality.
